cases.pattern_name: Shared infrastructure brushing and subsidy skimming
cases.assertion: AER-001 detects new-account subsidy skimming: shared devices/IPs/payment accounts, fast refunds, low-quality logistics, and weak normal-business counter explanations.
AER-002 detects reputation manipulation: repeated micro-orders, duplicate review text/images, automation signals, shared infrastructure, and merchant-level rating inflation.

cases.pattern_name: Promotion subsidy arbitrage and refund extraction
cases.assertion: AER-003 detects subsidy arbitrage: concentrated eligibility keys and payment accounts repeatedly extract high discounts with abnormal refund/logistics patterns.

observations.summary: Expanded entity-resolution graph across account, device, IP, payment, logistics, and evidence nodes.

support_evidence.description: 14 users and 80 orders share 3 devices; reuse ratio 26.7.
80 orders concentrate on 2 datacenter/proxy IP addresses.

nodes.label: EC-SKIM-001
nodes.text: AER-001 identifies potential subsidy skimming through shared infrastructure.

observations.summary: Refund cluster query compared case refunds with dynamic promotion cohort baseline.

support_evidence.description: 57 of 80 orders were refunded; lift is 12.7x cohort baseline and 32 refunds occurred within 24h.

observations.summary: Payment cluster query found concentrated payment instruments across supposedly independent buyers.

support_evidence.description: 80 payments collapse to 4 payment accounts; top account appears 20 times.

observations.summary: Counter-evidence search tested normal-business explanations before passport emission.

counter_evidence.description: No strong old-customer, campus, household, organic campaign, or warehouse-batch counter-evidence was found.

passport.risk_assertion: AER-001 identifies potential subsidy skimming through shared infrastructure.

support_evidence.description: 14 users and 80 orders share 3 devices; reuse ratio 26.7.
80 orders concentrate on 2 datacenter/proxy IP addresses.
80 payments collapse to 4 payment accounts; top account appears 20 times.
57 of 80 orders were refunded; lift is 12.7x cohort baseline and 32 refunds occurred within 24h.

counter_evidence.description: No strong old-customer, campus, household, organic campaign, or warehouse-batch counter-evidence was found.

remaining_uncertainty: Payment account ownership still requires review.
Warehouse batch shipping is only partially ruled out.
recommended_action: Expand investigation by querying logistics trace, comparing promo cohorts, analyzing behavior sequences, and seeking additional counter-evidence.

human_gate.reason: Evidence quality and disposition require auditor review.

passport.model_narrative: The case AER-001 involves a high-risk pattern (EC-SKIM-001) indicating potential subsidy skimming through shared infrastructure. The investigation has identified multiple entities involved, including numerous orders, users, devices, IPs, merchants, and payment accounts. High statistical and relational scores suggest strong correlations among these entities. Evidence supports significant device reuse, IP clustering, and payment aggregation, as well as abnormal refund rates. However, critical evidence such as logistics authenticity, promo cohort outliers, subsidy abuse, and behavior automation is missing.

observations.summary: Behavior-sequence analysis fused uploaded gateway logs, device logs, and review content similarity.

support_evidence.description: Uploaded device/gateway logs show average automation score 0.78 across 64 device events.
64 comments include 128 duplicate text/image reuse hits.

observations.summary: Payment cluster query found concentrated payment instruments across supposedly independent buyers.

support_evidence.description: 64 payments collapse to 7 payment accounts; top account appears 10 times.

observations.summary: Case memory retrieval compared current evidence against approved historical pattern trajectories.

support_evidence.description: Historical memory 'Review farm with verified micro orders' matches this case pattern: Prior reputation manipulation case: low-value orders, duplicate review images, high automation score, and few refunds.

observations.summary: Counter-evidence search tested normal-business explanations before passport emission.

counter_evidence.description: No strong old-customer, campus, household, organic campaign, or warehouse-batch counter-evidence was found.

passport.model_narrative: The investigation into potential reputation manipulation at AER-002 has identified strong evidence of repeated micro-orders, duplicate reviews, and automated behavior. The historical pattern match and review similarity support the assertion. However, there is a significant gap in evidence for device reuse, IP cluster analysis, and logistics authenticity, which could indicate false positives or missed indicators.

observations.summary: Subsidy ledger query linked promotion rules, eligibility keys, and repeated subsidy extraction.

support_evidence.description: 72 subsidy claims total 2997.75; repeated eligibility keys=15 and average subsidy=41.64.

observations.summary: Dynamic cohort comparison tested whether Black Friday traffic explains the anomaly.

support_evidence.description: Case composite promo risk 0.35 is 2.2x the matched category/promo cohort baseline 0.16.

observations.summary: Refund cluster query compared case refunds with dynamic promotion cohort baseline.

support_evidence.description: 29 of 72 orders were refunded; lift is 5.2x cohort baseline and 18 refunds occurred within 24h.

observations.summary: Counter-evidence search tested normal-business explanations before passport emission.

counter_evidence.description: No strong old-customer, campus, household, organic campaign, or warehouse-batch counter-evidence was found.

passport.model_narrative: The case AER-003 involves a high-risk pattern (EC-ARBI-003) related to promotion subsidy abuse and refund extraction. The investigation has identified several key pieces of evidence supporting this pattern, including abnormal refund rates, repeated subsidy claims, and concentrated device reuse. However, there is uncertainty regarding payment cluster analysis and lack of strong counter-evidence.
